conversations.say: Morgan, this ledger shows a recurring private habit, but it does not prove one customer or identify who used the R account. The export also shows several collectors and a disputed change date.
conversations.offer: I’ll show you O06 and explain the bounded transaction pattern.
conversations.request: In return, attribute the pattern to my records, omit customer names and side arrangements, and do not present it as proof of a single R.

conversations.say: Inez, I’m publicly forgiving the old R-associated balance instead of selling the neighborhood a cleaner story than the ledger supports.
conversations.offer: I’ll keep your side arrangements and local customer details private.
conversations.request: Promise the same boundary for my customers, and publicly support my decision to forgive the balance without claiming it identifies R.

public_statement: The outstanding R-associated account is forgiven. That closes the debt, not the mystery: my records show continuity, several collectors, and a disputed change, so nobody gets to turn this ledger into proof of one person.

commitments.promise: Use O06 only as a bounded transaction pattern, credit Rafi’s records, protect customer identities, and avoid claiming it proves a single R.
Mutually protect local customer identities and side arrangements.
Publicly support Rafi’s forgiveness of the outstanding account without treating the ledger as proof of R’s identity.

correction: An R-associated account does not identify its sender, and recurring behavior does not establish one person.
goal_progress: Deliberately forgave the outstanding account in public.
Asked C16 for public neighborhood support and a mutual disclosure boundary.
Offered C14 bounded ledger access for attribution and privacy limits.
reasoning_summary: Rafi chooses neighborhood control over an embellished continuity sale: close the debt publicly, seek Inez’s reciprocal protection and support, and trade Morgan only a carefully limited pattern.
